| **Plan** | Identify the problem, analyse data, develop a hypothesis | Define objectives, processes, resources |
| **Do** | Implement the plan on a small scale (pilot) | Execute the change, document observations |
| **Check** | Measure results against expected outcomes | Verify effectiveness using statistical tools |
| **Act** | Standardize the improvement or restart the cycle | Adopt, abandon, or revise the change |

> [!NOTE]
> PDCA is sometimes called the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**. The KTU exam often tests the **sequence** and the **purpose of each stage** — students lose marks by describing steps in the wrong order.

## 2.2 Deming's 14 Points — Strategic Quality Leadership

Deming's 14 Points for Management form the **strategic backbone** of TQM. They are often asked as a **7-mark structured question** in KTU ESE.

1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

## 2.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy

Juran reduced quality management to **three universal processes**, applicable to *any* industry:

$$\text{Quality Management} = \text{Quality Planning} + \text{Quality Control} + \text{Quality Improvement}$$

| Trilogy Stage | Purpose | Key Output |
|----------------|---------|------------|
| **Quality Planning** | Identify customers and their needs | Quality goals, metrics |
| **Quality Control** | Evaluate actual vs. planned performance | Process stability |
| **Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement | Breakthrough projects |

## 2.4 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality

Crosby argued that quality management is built on **four absolutes** (no exceptions):

1. **Quality = Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness")
2. **Prevention, not inspection** (the system must prevent defects)
3. **Zero defects performance standard** (not "acceptable quality levels")
4. **Measure quality by the price of non-conformance** (P.O.N.C.)

> [!NOTE]
> Crosby's **Price of Non-Conformance (P.O.N.C.)** is a key financial metric:
> $$\text{P.O.N.C.} = \text{Cost of rework} + \text{Scrap} + \text{Warranty} + \text{Lost goodwill} + \text{Re-inspection}$$
> Compare with **Price of Conformance (P.O.C.)** = cost of doing things *right the first time*.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Key TQM & Quality Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU ESE — Memorize These Definitions and Equations**

| # | Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Lower is better. Six Sigma target $\le$ 3.4 DPMO. |
| 2 | **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | $C_p \ge 1.33$ is industry standard |
| 3 | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ indicates capable process |
| 4 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing one process}}{\text{Total units entering}} \times 100\%$ | Measures process efficiency |
| 5 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Product of all process yields |
| 6 | **Sigma Level** | $\sigma_{level} = \text{NORMSINV}(1 - \text{DPMO}/10^{6}) + 1.5$ | Higher sigma = fewer defects |
| 7 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $\text{CoQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Expressed as % of sales |
| 8 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Orders delivered on time}}{\text{Total orders}} \times 100\%$ | World-class: $\ge$ 98% |
| 9 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | World-class: $\ge$ 85% |
| 10 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100\%$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Markdown Safety:** All vertical-bar expressions use `\vert` style (e.g., $\text{USL} - \mu \over 3\sigma$) — never the raw `|` character, to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.6 Six Sigma — Data-Driven Quality Excellence

**Six Sigma** is a *disciplined, data-driven methodology* developed by Motorola (1986) and popularized by **General Electric** under Jack Welch. It seeks to reduce process variation so that only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** occur — translating to **4.5 sigma performance** after a 1.5-sigma shift correction.

### The DMAIC Framework

| Phase | Meaning | Focus |
|-------|---------|-------|
| **D** | Define | Customer, problem, project scope (CTQ — Critical to Quality) |
| **M** | Measure | Current process performance, baseline sigma |
| **A** | Analyze | Root-cause analysis (Fishbone, Pareto, Regression) |
| **I** | Improve | Pilot solutions, optimize |
| **C** | Control | Statistical Process Control (SPC), documentation |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently frames **DMAIC vs. DMADV** as a *compare-and-contrast* question. **DMAIC** improves existing processes; **DMADV** (Define–Measure–Analyze–Design–Verify) is used to *design* new processes or products to Six Sigma levels.

## 2.7 The Seven Tools of Quality (Old / Ishikawa's Tools)

These are the **statistical problem-solving tools** expected in any TQM question:

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Fishbone / Ishikawa)
2. **Check Sheet**
3. **Control Chart**
4. **Histogram**
5. **Pareto Chart** (80/20 rule)
6. **Scatter Diagram**
7. **Flowchart / Stratification**

> [!NOTE]
> Often phrased in exams as *"List and explain any five tools of quality"* (5 marks) or *"Apply Pareto analysis to..."* (numerical 7 marks).

## 2.8 Benchmarking — Learning from the Best

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmarking** is the *continuous, systematic process of measuring one's products, services, and practices against recognized industry leaders* to identify gaps and implement improvements.

### Types of Benchmarking

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Internal** | Compare between units within the same organization | Two plants of the same company |
| **Competitive** | Compare with direct competitors | Toyota vs. Honda assembly lines |
| **Functional** | Compare with best-in-class in the same function | Procurement practice of a logistics firm |
| **Generic** | Compare with world-class leaders regardless of industry | Hospital adopting airline check-in process |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation

> **Problem:** A semiconductor company produced **12,000 integrated circuits** last month. Each IC has **80 opportunities for defects** (bonding, lithography, packaging, etc.). The total defects found in final inspection were **240**. Calculate:
> 1. The **DPMO**
> 2. The **Sigma Level** (assume a 1.5-sigma shift)
> 3. Comment on whether the process meets Six Sigma standards.

### Step 1 — Compute the DPMO

We use the standard DPMO formula:

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$$

**Substituting the given values:**

- Number of Defects = **240**
- Number of Units = **12,000**
- Opportunities per Unit = **80**

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{240}{12{,}000 \times 80} \times 10^{6}$$

**Compute the denominator first:**

$$\begin{aligned}
12{,}000 \times 80 &= 960{,}000 \\
\frac{240}{960{,}000} &= 0.00025 \\
0.00025 \times 10^{6} &= 250
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 250 \text{ defects per million opportunities}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Sigma Level (with 1.5-sigma shift correction)

The standard formula (using NORMSINV in spreadsheet tools):

$$\sigma_{level} = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5$$

**Step 2a — Compute the inverse-CDF argument:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} &= \frac{250}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.00025 \\
1 - 0.00025 &= 0.99975
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2b — Evaluate the standard normal inverse:**

$$\text{NORMSINV}(0.99975) \approx 3.49$$

**Step 2c — Add the 1.5-sigma long-term drift:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{level} &= 3.49 + 1.5 \\
&= 4.99 \approx 5.0 \text{ sigma}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{level} \approx 5.0 \text{ sigma}}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

| Benchmark | DPMO | Sigma Level |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| Industry Average (3-sigma) | 66,807 | 3.0 |
| World-Class (Six Sigma) | 3.4 | 6.0 |
| **Our Company** | **250** | **5.0** |
| World-Class Target | $\le$ 3.4 | 6.0 |

**Conclusion:** The process is performing at **5-sigma** level — *better than industry average* but **not yet at Six Sigma**. To achieve Six Sigma, the company must **reduce DPMO by ~74×** (from 250 to 3.4), typically via **DMAIC** projects targeting top defect categories identified by a **Pareto chart**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

> **Problem:** A 4-stage manufacturing line produces printed circuit boards. The First Pass Yields (FPY) at each stage are:
>
> | Stage | FPY |
> |-------|-----|
> | Solder Paste Printing | 0.98 |
> | Pick-and-Place | 0.96 |
> | Reflow Soldering | 0.95 |
> | Final Test | 0.92 |
>
> Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and interpret the result.

### Step 1 — Recall the RTY Formula

$$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$$

### Step 2 — Multiply Step-by-Step

**Stage 1 → Stage 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY}_{1-2} &= 0.98 \times 0.96 \\
&= 0.9408
\end{aligned}$$

**Stage 1 → 3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY}_{1-3} &= 0.9408 \times 0.95 \\
&= 0.89376
\end{aligned}$$

**Stage 1 → 4 (final RTY):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY}_{1-4} &= 0.89376 \times 0.92 \\
&= 0.8222592
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{RTY} \approx 0.8223 \text{ or } 82.23\%}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

Although each individual stage's FPY looks reasonable ($\ge$ 92%), the **cumulative yield** drops to **~82%**. This means **~17.7% of boards require rework or are scrapped** somewhere in the line.

**Pareto Insight (KTU Exam Tip):** Identify the *weakest* stage. Here, **Final Test (FPY = 0.92)** and **Reflow (0.95)** are the bottlenecks — focus DMAIC efforts there for maximum yield improvement.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Process Capability ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$)

> **Problem:** A CNC lathe produces shafts with specification limits: $\text{USL} = 12.10$ mm, $\text{LSL} = 11.90$ mm. The process has mean $\mu = 12.00$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.025$ mm. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and comment on process capability.

### Step 1 — Compute $C_p$

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{12.10 - 11.90}{6 \times 0.025} \\
&= \frac{0.20}{0.15} \\
&= 1.333
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{C_p = 1.333}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Upper side:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} &= \frac{12.10 - 12.00}{3 \times 0.025} \\
&= \frac{0.10}{0.075} \\
&= 1.333
\end{aligned}$$

**Lower side:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} &= \frac{12.00 - 11.90}{3 \times 0.025} \\
&= \frac{0.10}{0.075} \\
&= 1.333
\end{aligned}$$

**Take the minimum:**

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = 1.333}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation Matrix

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Capability Rating | KTU Exam Standard Answer |
|----------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| $< 1.00$ | Process **incapable** | Produces defects, not acceptable |
| $1.00 \le C_{pk} < 1.33$ | **Marginally capable** | Needs improvement |
| $1.33 \le C_{pk} < 1.67$ | **Capable** | Acceptable, monitor |
| $\ge 1.67$ | **Highly capable / Six Sigma** | World-class |

**Conclusion:** The CNC lathe is **capable** ($C_{pk} = 1.33$) and the process is **perfectly centered** ($C_p = C_{pk}$). To reach Six Sigma, the company must either **halve $\sigma$** or **double specification width** — typically achieved by reducing machine variation (TPM, better tooling, SPC).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — TQM Frameworks Comparison

| Framework | Primary Focus | Origin | Methodology | Best Use Case |
|-----------|---------------|--------|--------------|---------------|
| **TQM** | Organization-wide culture | Feigenbaum (1956) | PDCA + principles | Long-term cultural shift |
| **Six Sigma** | Variation reduction | Motorola (1986) | DMAIC / DMADV | Process excellence projects |
| **Lean** | Waste elimination | Toyota (1950s) | Value-stream mapping | Production efficiency |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous small improvements | Japan (1985) | Gemba walks, suggestions | Employee-driven changes |
| **BPR** | Radical redesign | Hammer & Champy (1993) | Process re-engineering | Transformational change |
| **5S** | Workplace organization | Toyota (1980s) | Sort-Set-Shine-Standardize-Sustain | Foundation for all above |
| **TPM** | Equipment effectiveness | Japan (1971) | Autonomous maintenance | Manufacturing reliability |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answers, always state the **origin**, **key methodology**, and **one distinguishing feature** of any framework. Marks are awarded for *each* dimension.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TQM House Model — Architecture of Quality

The TQM *House* is the canonical visual representation used in KTU 2024 Module-3 notes. It depicts TQM as a building with a **roof** (customer satisfaction), **walls** (tools, methods), and **foundation** (leadership, culture).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Foundation
        F1["LEADERSHIP AND COMMITMENT"]
        F2["CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT CULTURE"]
        F3["TRAINING AND EDUCATION"]
        F4["EMPLOYEE INVOLVEMENT"]
    end

    subgraph Pillars
        P1["PROCESS MANAGEMENT"]
        P2["DATA AND ANALYSIS"]
        P3["SUPPLIER PARTNERSHIPS"]
        P4["CUSTOMER FOCUS"]
    end

    subgraph Tools
        T1["SEVEN QC TOOLS"]
        T2["PDCA CYCLE"]
        T3["DMAIC FRAMEWORK"]
        T4["BENCHMARKING"]
    end

    R["CUSTOMER SATISFACTION AND BUSINESS RESULTS"]

    F1 --> P1
    F2 --> P1
    F3 --> T1
    F4 --> T3
    P1 --> T1
    P2 --> T2
    P3 --> T4
    P4 --> T3
    T1 --> R
    T2 --> R
    T3 --> R
    T4 --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading:** The **roof** (Customer Satisfaction) is held up by **pillars** (process, data, suppliers, customer focus), supported by the **foundation** (leadership, culture, training, people). Remove the foundation and the entire structure collapses — this is *why* KTU stresses **leadership commitment** as the #1 success factor.

## 4.2 PDCA Cycle — Continuous Improvement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PLAN<br/>Define problem<br/>Analyse data<br/>Set targets"]
    B["DO<br/>Pilot implementation<br/>Execute small scale<br/>Document results"]
    C["CHECK<br/>Measure outcomes<br/>Compare with targets<br/>Statistical analysis"]
    D["ACT<br/>Standardize if success<br/>Or restart cycle<br/>Adopt improvements"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students draw PDCA as a *straight line* (Plan → Do → Check → Act → End). The correct topology is a **closed feedback loop** with no end point — improvements restart the cycle indefinitely.

## 4.3 DMAIC Flow — Six Sigma Improvement Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["DEFINE<br/>Voice of customer<br/>CTQ tree<br/>Project charter"]
    M1["MEASURE<br/>Baseline sigma<br/>DPMO calculation<br/>Data collection plan"]
    A1["ANALYZE<br/>Root cause analysis<br/>Pareto and fishbone<br/>Hypothesis testing"]
    I1["IMPROVE<br/>Pilot solutions<br/>Design of experiments<br/>Optimize parameters"]
    C1["CONTROL<br/>SPC charts<br/>Mistake proofing<br/>Standardize"]

    D1 --> M1
    M1 --> A1
    A1 --> I1
    I1 --> C1
    C1 -.Feedback.-> D1
```

## 4.4 Manufacturing Excellence Pyramid — Strategic Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["LEVEL 1<br/>Customer Satisfaction and Profitability"]
    L2["LEVEL 2<br/>World Class Manufacturing and Six Sigma"]
    L3["LEVEL 3<br/>Lean Systems and JIT"]
    L4["LEVEL 4<br/>TQM and Continuous Improvement"]
    L5["LEVEL 5<br/>Foundation: People, Processes, Infrastructure"]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Bottom-Up Reading:** A factory must first *build the foundation* (people, infrastructure) → *adopt TQM culture* → *implement Lean* → *pursue Six Sigma* → *achieve WCM* → *deliver customer satisfaction and profit*. Skipping a level typically results in *unsustainable* excellence programs.

## 4.5 Fishbone Diagram — Generic TQM Root-Cause Template

```mermaid
fishbone
    --> M1["Measurement Systems"]
    --> M2["Methods"]
    --> M3["Manpower"]
    --> M4["Materials"]
    --> M5["Machines"]
    --> M6["Environment"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the classic **6M Ishikawa Fishbone**. In a KTU 7-mark question, students are expected to (a) draw the structure, (b) populate it for a *specific problem* (e.g., high defect rate in PCB assembly), and (c) identify the dominant branch using Pareto data.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four of its core principles.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Total Quality Management (TQM) is an integrated, organization-wide management philosophy focused on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all employees — from top management to shop-floor — to meet or exceed customer expectations.

**Any four of the eight ISO-based principles (2 marks):**

1. **Customer Focus** — Primary focus is meeting customer needs and expectations.
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Involvement of People** — Competent, empowered people at all levels are the core of the organization.
4. **Process Approach** — Desired results are achieved efficiently when activities are managed as interrelated processes.

*(Other acceptable principles: System Approach, Continuous Improvement, Factual Decision-Making, Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships.)*

---

### Question 2: What is a Quality Circle? Mention its key features.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A **Quality Circle** is a small group of workers (typically 6–12 members) who do the same or similar work, meeting regularly to identify, analyse, and solve work-related quality, productivity, and safety problems. The concept was pioneered by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in Japan in 1962.

**Key Features (1.5 marks):**

1. **Voluntary membership** — Workers join willingly; not assigned.
2. **Small group size** — Usually 6 to 12 members to ensure participation.
3. **Same work area** — Members come from the same shop or department for domain knowledge.
4. **Continuous improvement focus** — Targets ongoing small problems using TQM tools.
5. **Self-development** — Builds leadership, problem-solving, and team skills.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Module-3 carries 14 marks. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** is given. Each option has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a) Explain Deming's 14 Points for Management in detail. How do they contribute to building a TQM culture? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
W. Edwards Deming's 14 Points for Management, published in his landmark book *Out of the Crisis* (1982), represent a transformative philosophy for organizational management. They form the strategic core of TQM implementation.

**Categorization of the 14 Points (4 marks):**

For a structured answer, group the 14 points into **five clusters**:

**Cluster 1 — Purpose and Philosophy (Points 1, 2):**
- **Point 1: Create constancy of purpose for improvement.** Innovation, research, and long-term planning must replace short-term profits.
- **Point 2: Adopt the new philosophy.** Reject the tolerance of delays, mistakes, and defective workmanship.

**Cluster 2 — Quality over Inspection and Price (Points 3, 4):**
- **Point 3: Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.** Build quality *into the process* from the start; inspection is costly and inefficient.
- **Point 4: End the practice of awarding business on the lowest price tag.** Reward quality and dependability; long-term loyalty reduces total cost.

**Cluster 3 — Continuous Improvement and Process Discipline (Points 5, 6, 7, 8, 9):**
- **Point 5: Improve constantly every process.** Use PDCA to enhance quality, productivity, and cost.
- **Point 6: Institute training on the job.** Workers must understand their processes and variation.
- **Point 7: Institute leadership.** Supervisors must lead, not just supervise.
- **Point 8: Drive out fear.** Fear destroys innovation and engagement.
- **Point 9: Break down barriers between departments.** Team-based problem solving replaces silos.

**Cluster 4 — Eliminate Slogans and Quotas (Points 10, 11, 12):**
- **Point 10: Eliminate slogans and exhortations** without a system behind them.
- **Point 11: Eliminate numerical quotas** that create conflict and short-cuts.
- **Point 12: Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.** Workers should feel pride in their craft.

**Cluster 5 — Education and Transformation (Points 13, 14):**
- **Point 13: Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement** for all.
- **Point 14: Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.** TQM is *everyone's* job.

**Contribution to TQM Culture (2 marks):**

- Establishes **leadership commitment** at the top — Point 1 and 2.
- Promotes **process thinking** over inspection-based control — Point 3, 5.
- Encourages **statistical thinking and learning** — Points 5, 6, 13.
- Fosters **employee empowerment** — Points 8, 9, 12.
- Builds a **long-term transformation mindset** — Points 1, 14.

Deming's 14 Points provide a **moral and operational compass** for any organization seeking to embed TQM — they are not tactics but *philosophical commandments*.

---

**(b) A manufacturing company produces 5,000 automotive parts per month. Each part has 25 opportunities for defects. In one month, 350 defects were observed. Calculate the DPMO, the Sigma Level (with 1.5-sigma shift), and recommend whether the process is suitable for Six Sigma certification. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Number of units = 5,000
- Opportunities per unit = 25
- Number of defects = 350

**Step 1: DPMO Calculation (2 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^{6}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{350}{5{,}000 \times 25} \times 10^{6}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= 125{,}000 \\
\frac{350}{125{,}000} &= 0.0028 \\
\text{DPMO} &= 2{,}800
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 2{,}800}$$

**Step 2: Sigma Level Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{level} = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(1 - \frac{2{,}800}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5$$

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - 0.0028 &= 0.9972 \\
\text{NORMSINV}(0.9972) &\approx 2.78 \\
\sigma_{level} &= 2.78 + 1.5 = 4.28
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{level} \approx 4.3 \text{ sigma}}$$

**Step 3: Comparison & Recommendation (3 marks):**

| Benchmark | DPMO | Sigma |
|-----------|------|-------|
| **Our Process** | 2,800 | 4.3 |
| Industry Average (3-sigma) | 66,807 | 3.0 |
| Six Sigma Target | 3.4 | 6.0 |

**Recommendation:**
- The process is **better than industry average** (4.3 vs 3.0 sigma) but is **not Six Sigma**.
- To achieve Six Sigma, DPMO must drop from **2,800 → 3.4** (a **~823×** improvement).
- The company should:
  1. Launch a **DMAIC project** to identify root causes using **Pareto** and **Fishbone** analysis.
  2. Implement **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** to detect and prevent variation.
  3. Provide **workforce training** in statistical thinking.
  4. Adopt **mistake-proofing (Poka-Yoke)** to eliminate error opportunities.
- **Conclusion:** The process is **not yet Six Sigma** but has a *strong improvement potential* within 2–3 DMAIC cycles.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a) Explain Juran's Quality Trilogy. Compare it with Deming's PDCA cycle. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Juran's Quality Trilogy (4 marks):**
Juran defined quality management as **three interrelated processes** applicable to *any* function:

1. **Quality Planning (1.3 marks):**
   - Identify customers (internal and external).
   - Determine customer needs (CTQs).
   - Develop product/process features that meet those needs.
   - Establish quality goals and metrics.

2. **Quality Control (1.3 marks):**
   - Evaluate actual performance against goals.
   - Compare actual vs. planned outcomes.
   - Take corrective action on the difference (gap).
   - Use SPC, control charts.

3. **Quality Improvement (1.4 marks):**
   - Establish infrastructure for *breakthrough* projects.
   - Identify projects with highest ROI.
   - Provide training, resources, and review committees.
   - Use team-based problem solving.

**Comparison with Deming's PDCA (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Juran's Trilogy | Deming's PDCA |
|--------|------------------|----------------|
| **Structure** | Three parallel processes (Planning, Control, Improvement) | Four-stage cycle (Plan, Do, Check, Act) |
| **Focus** | Management responsibility and quality project portfolio | Continuous iterative improvement of any process |
| **Scope** | Macro-quality strategy | Micro-process improvement |
| **Improvement Type** | Distinguishes *control* (holding the gain) from *improvement* (breakthrough) | Single cycle; assumes gains are standardized in *Act* |
| **Customer** | Explicit customer-needs stage in Quality Planning | Implicit — emerges from problem analysis |
| **Output** | Quality system design | Iterative process improvement |

**Conclusion:** Juran's Trilogy is *managerial and structural*; Deming's PDCA is *operational and iterative*. Both are **complementary** — Juran's *Planning* maps to *Plan*, *Control* maps to *Check*, and *Improvement* maps to the *Do* + *Act* loop of Deming.

---

**(b) Discuss the concept of World Class Manufacturing (WCM). List and explain the pillars of WCM with reference to a real-world manufacturing case. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of WCM (1.5 marks):**
**World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is a comprehensive operational philosophy that integrates **TQM, Lean Manufacturing, Six Sigma, TPM, and JIT** into a single coherent system to achieve *excellence across quality, cost, delivery, safety, and morale*. WCM is typically measured against a *12-pillar model* developed by **Schonberger** and refined at **Fiat**, **Brevini**, and **Toyota**.

**The 12 Pillars of WCM (4 marks):**

| # | Pillar | Focus |
|---|--------|-------|
| 1 | **Safety** | Zero-accident workplace |
| 2 | **Cost Deployment** | Cost reduction by Pareto analysis |
| 3 | **Focused Improvement (Kaizen)** | Continuous incremental improvements |
| 4 | **Autonomous Maintenance** | Operators maintain their own machines |
| 5 | **Professional Maintenance** | Planned, predictive maintenance by experts |
| 6 | **Quality Control** | Zero defects, mistake-proofing |
| 7 | **Logistics & Customer Service** | JIT delivery, short lead times |
| 8 | **Supplier Partnership** | Long-term, integrated supplier development |
| 9 | **People Development** | Skills, training, and engagement |
| 10 | **Workplace Organization (5S)** | Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain |
| 11 | **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-proofing devices |
| 12 | **Process & Product Management** | Concurrent engineering, design for manufacture |

**Real-World Case — Toyota Production System (1.5 marks):**
Toyota is the *gold standard* of WCM. The Toyota Production System (TPS) integrates all 12 pillars:
- **Pillar 1 (Safety):** Zero-accident goal since the 1960s.
- **Pillar 4 (Autonomous Maintenance):** Operators perform daily cleaning, lubrication, and inspection — *"The machine is the operator's responsibility."*
- **Pillar 6 (Quality Control):** **Jidoka (autonomation)** — any worker can stop the line by pulling the *Andon* cord when a defect is suspected.
- **Pillar 7 (Logistics):** **Just-in-Time (JIT)** — parts arrive *exactly when needed*, eliminating inventory.
- **Pillar 10 (5S):** Standard foundation for shop-floor discipline.

**Conclusion (extra):** WCM is the *strategic destination* that a manufacturing enterprise aims for. The journey begins with **TQM**, proceeds through **Lean and Six Sigma**, and culminates in **WCM certification** through a multi-year transformation programme.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in TQM & Manufacturing Excellence Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing TQM with Quality Control (QC):** Many students write *"TQM is inspection of products"* — this loses 1–2 marks immediately. TQM is *prevention-based, organization-wide culture*; QC is *end-of-line defect detection*.
>
> 2. **Listing gurus without contributions:** Writing *"Deming, Juran, Crosby"* without stating *what they did* earns 0 marks. Always pair the name with the concept (e.g., *"Deming — 14 Points, PDCA"*).
>
> 3. **Misinterpreting PDCA sequence:** Drawing Plan → Act → Do → Check is an *instant 0* for that sub-question. The correct order is **Plan → Do → Check → Act**, with **Act feeding back to Plan**.
>
> 4. **Numerical errors in DPMO:** Forgetting the multiplication by $10^6$ in DPMO is a frequent mistake. Always write the **full formula** in the *first step* before substituting.
>
> 5. **Mixing DMAIC and DMADV:** DMAIC = *improve existing process*; DMADV = *design new process*. Examiners penalize confusion between the two.
>
> 6. **Omitting real-world examples:** KTU 2024 rewards *application-level* answers. Always tie theory to a *concrete industry example* (Toyota, GE, Motorola, Apollo Hospitals, Tata Steel).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — TQM & Manufacturing Excellence (Module 3)**

### A. Core Definitions to Memorize
- **TQM** — Comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy focusing on customer satisfaction through continuous improvement and people involvement.
- **Manufacturing Excellence** — Sustained superior performance across quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, and innovation.
- **Six Sigma** — Data-driven methodology targeting $\le$ **3.4 DPMO** (4.5-sigma after 1.5 shift).
- **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** — Integration of TQM, Lean, Six Sigma, TPM, JIT into a unified 12-pillar system.
- **PDCA** — **P**lan → **D**o → **C**heck → **A**ct (closed-loop, never-ending).
- **DMAIC** — **D**efine → **M**easure → **A**nalyze → **I**mprove → **C**ontrol.
- **DMADV** — **D**efine → **M**easure → **A**nalyze → **D**esign → **V**erify (for new designs).
- **Quality Circle** — Small voluntary group of workers solving quality problems (Ishikawa).
- **Benchmarking** — Continuous comparison against industry best practices.
- **Jidoka** — Toyota's principle of *automation with a human touch* (autonomation).

### B. Gurus and Their Must-Know Concepts
- **Deming** → 14 Points, PDCA, System of Profound Knowledge, Statistical Process Control
- **Juran** → Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement), *Fitness for Use*
- **Crosby** → Zero Defects, 4 Absolutes, P.O.N.C. (Price of Non-Conformance)
- **Ishikawa** → Quality Circles, Fishbone Diagram
- **Feigenbaum** → Total Quality Control (origin of TQ concept)
- **Taguchi** → Loss Function, Robust Design

### C. The Eight TQM Principles (ISO Foundation)
Customer focus, Leadership, Involvement, Process approach, System approach, Continuous improvement, Factual decision-making, Mutually beneficial supplier relationships.

### D. The Seven Tools of Quality (Ishikawa)
Cause-and-effect diagram, Check sheet, Control chart, Histogram, Pareto chart, Scatter diagram, Flowchart.

### E. Key Formulas (Memorize Numerator, Denominator, Units)
- **DPMO** = $\dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$
- **$C_p$** = $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$
- **$C_{pk}$** = $\min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$
- **RTY** = $\prod \text{FPY}_i$
- **OEE** = Availability $\times$ Performance $\times$ Quality

### F. Five Critical Numerical Benchmarks
- 3-sigma = 66,807 DPMO (industry average)
- 4-sigma = 621 DPMO
- 5-sigma = 233 DPMO
- 6-sigma = 3.4 DPMO (world class)
- World-class $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$

### G. Top 5 Frameworks to Compare
TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, Kaizen, BPR — know the *origin, focus, methodology,* and *one distinguishing feature* of each.

### H. The Three Pillars of Manufacturing Excellence
1. **Quality Excellence** (Six Sigma, TQM, SPC)
2. **Operational Excellence** (Lean, JIT, TPM)
3. **Strategic Excellence** (Benchmarking, WCM, Agile Manufacturing)

### I. Common 14-Mark Question Patterns (Practice These)
1. *Explain TQM principles with reference to [guru's] contributions.*
2. *Discuss Six Sigma — DMAIC framework with a numerical example.*
3. *Compare TQM and Six Sigma / Lean Manufacturing.*
4. *Explain World Class Manufacturing (WCM) with case study.*
5. *Deming's 14 Points — explain with reference to Indian manufacturing context.*

### J. The One-Line Mantra for Examiners
> *"TQM is not a tool — it is a philosophy. Six Sigma is not a certification — it is a data-driven journey. Manufacturing Excellence is not a destination — it is a continuous pursuit of world-class performance, rooted in culture, measured by outcomes, and driven by people."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deming's Approach to Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT723 - MANUFACTURING STRATEGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deming's Approach to Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Deming's Approach to Quality** is a comprehensive, management-centered philosophy of quality improvement developed by Dr. W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993), an American engineer, statistician, and management consultant. It is a holistic system that emphasizes **continuous improvement**, **statistical thinking**, **systems optimization**, and **transformation of organizational culture** rather than mere inspection or short-term profit maximization.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT723 – Manufacturing Strategy)**, Deming's Approach is studied as a foundational pillar of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and strategic quality planning. It positions quality not as a departmental function but as an organization-wide strategic imperative driven by leadership.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Deming's philosophy is treated as a *management system*, not a *toolbox*. The examiner tests whether the student understands the **interconnection** between his 14 Points, the **PDCA Cycle**, the **System of Profound Knowledge**, and the **Chain Reaction** — not just rote memorization of bullet points.

The core tenets can be formally stated as:

> *"Quality is not an act, it is a habit."* — paraphrased from Deming's teaching philosophy, emphasizing that sustainable quality emerges from a deeply embedded organizational culture, not from isolated inspection activities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — "The Gardener and the Orchard":**

Imagine you inherit an apple orchard. Two managers give you advice:

- **Traditional Manager**: "Hire workers to sort the apples after harvest. Throw away the bad ones. Only ship the good ones."
- **Deming**: "Don't focus on sorting rotten apples. Instead, study the **soil**, the **climate variability**, the **rootstock genetics**, and the **watering system**. Improve the **entire ecosystem** so the tree naturally produces better apples every season."

Deming's approach is the second. He argued that **94% of quality problems** are caused by the **system and management**, while only **6%** are attributable to individual workers. Therefore, the solution lies in **fixing the system**, not blaming the worker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics in Deming's Approach (Bolded Standard Values):**
> - **94% of failures** — attributable to system/process (management controllable)
> - **6% of failures** — attributable to workers
> - **PDCA Cycle** — iterative improvement loop
> - **14 Points** — actionable management obligations
> - **4 Components** of the System of Profound Knowledge

## 1.3 Why Deming Matters in Manufacturing Strategy

Deming's ideas revolutionized Japanese manufacturing post-WWII (notably at **Toyota**), and later Western manufacturing. In KTU's strategic framework, his approach links directly to:

1. **Competitive positioning** through quality leadership
2. **Cost reduction** via variation reduction
3. **Customer value creation** as a strategic outcome
4. **Supplier partnership** as an extension of the system

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Components of Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)

Deming argued that to transform an organization, leaders must master four interrelated bodies of knowledge:

### 2.1.1 Appreciation for a System
- A **system** is a network of interdependent components working together to achieve an **aim**.
- Every organization is a system with **optimization** of the whole, not maximization of parts.
- In manufacturing: Procurement → Production → Assembly → Distribution must be optimized jointly.

### 2.1.2 Knowledge of Variation
- **Common cause variation** (inherent to the system) vs. **Special cause variation** (external disruptions).
- Tampering — reacting to common cause as if it were special cause — worsens the system.
- Statistical control is essential to distinguish the two.

### 2.1.3 Theory of Knowledge
- Knowledge is built through **observation, theory, and prediction**.
- Operational definitions must be clear; without them, data is meaningless.
- Experience alone is not knowledge — it must be tested against theory.

### 2.1.4 Psychology
- People are motivated by **intrinsic factors**: pride in workmanship, achievement, esteem, and growth.
- Fear and extrinsic punishment destroy quality.
- Management must understand human motivation to design systems that work.

## 2.2 Deming's 14 Points for Management

These are **obligations** of top management, not optional suggestions:

| # | Point | Strategic Intent |
|---|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service | Long-term vision over short-term profit |
| 2 | Adopt the new philosophy | Cultural transformation |
| 3 | Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality | Build quality in, not inspect it in |
| 4 | End the practice of awarding business on price alone | Total cost, not purchase price |
| 5 | Improve constantly every process for planning, production, and service | Continuous improvement (Kaizen) |
| 6 | Institute training on the job | Workforce capability development |
| 7 | Institute leadership | Supervision aimed at helping people do a better job |
| 8 | Drive out fear | Enable open communication |
| 9 | Break down barriers between departments | Cross-functional collaboration |
| 10 | Eliminate slogans and exhortations | Replace with systemic improvement |
| 11 | Eliminate numerical quotas | Remove arbitrary targets |
| 12 | Remove barriers to pride of workmanship | Allow autonomy and craftsmanship |
| 13 | Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement | Lifelong learning culture |
| 14 | Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation | Organization-wide commitment |

## 2.3 Deming's PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle)

The **Plan–Do–Check–Act** cycle is the operational engine of continuous improvement:

| Phase | Action | Key Question Answered |
|-------|--------|------------------------|
| **P (Plan)** | Identify problem, analyze process, hypothesize improvement | "What change will we make?" |
| **D (Do)** | Implement on small scale, collect data | "What happens when we try it?" |
| **C (Check)** | Evaluate results against hypothesis using statistical tools | "Did the change work?" |
| **A (Act)** | Standardize if successful, restart cycle if not | "What do we do next?" |

> [!NOTE]
> The PDCA cycle is **iterative** and **cumulative** — each rotation raises the performance baseline.

## 2.4 Deming's Chain Reaction

Deming proposed a cause-and-effect chain linking quality improvements to business outcomes:

$$\text{Improve Quality} \rightarrow \text{Decrease Costs} \rightarrow \text{Increase Productivity} \rightarrow \text{Secure Market} \rightarrow \text{Stay in Business} \rightarrow \text{Provide Jobs}$$

This chain contradicts the conventional belief that **higher quality = higher cost**, proving instead that quality is the primary driver of **cost reduction**.

## 2.5 Seven Deadly Diseases of Management (Obstacles to Quality)

Deming identified systemic dysfunctions that destroy quality culture:

1. **Lack of constancy of purpose**
2. **Emphasis on short-term profits**
3. **Evaluation by performance, merit rating, or annual review**
4. **Mobility of management** (job-hopping executives)
5. **Running a company on visible figures alone** (intangibles ignored)
6. **Excessive medical costs**
7. **Excessive costs of warranty, fueled by lawyers who work on contingency fees**

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Principle | Strategic Application |
|---------|-----------|------------------------|
| **94/6 Rule** | System causes = 94%, Worker causes = 6% | Fix the system, not blame workers |
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan–Do–Check–Act (iterative) | Continuous improvement engine |
| **14 Points** | Management obligations | Strategic quality framework |
| **4 SoPK Components** | System, Variation, Knowledge, Psychology | Leadership knowledge base |
| **Chain Reaction** | Quality → Cost↓ → Productivity↑ → Market Share↑ | Strategic value proposition |
| **Common Cause Variation** | Inherent system noise | Reduce by process redesign |
| **Special Cause Variation** | External, assignable disturbance | Remove by local intervention |
| **Tampering** | Reacting to common cause as special | Anti-pattern, worsens system |
| **Constancy of Purpose** | Long-term mission | Anti-short-termism |
| **Operational Definition** | Clear, communicable meaning of a concept | Foundation of statistical thinking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:** Deming's framework underpins modern **Six Sigma, Lean Manufacturing, ISO 9001 Quality Management Systems, and the Toyota Production System (TPS)**. For KTU students in Mechanical/Production/Industrial Engineering, the statistical variation concepts link directly to **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: Deming's PDCA in Manufacturing Context

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Scenario: Applying PDCA to Reduce Defect Rate

**Problem Statement:** A KTU manufacturing plant produces ball bearings. The current defect rate is **8%**. Management wants to apply Deming's PDCA to systematically reduce it. Demonstrate the analytical application of each phase.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — PLAN

Define the problem operationally. Establish an **operational definition** of a defect (e.g., "a bearing is defective if its diameter deviates more than **0.05 mm** from the nominal value of **10.00 mm**").

$$\text{Tolerance} = \pm 0.05 \text{ mm around nominal } 10.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Acceptance Range} = [9.95, 10.05] \text{ mm}$$

Hypothesize the cause. Using a **Pareto chart** of defects over a sample of $n = 500$ bearings, it is found that **60% of defects** are due to grinding machine vibration (a common cause).

$$\text{Defect contribution from grinding} = 0.60 \times 8\% = 4.8\%$$

Plan a change: Re-calibrate the grinding machine and install vibration dampers.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — DO

Implement the change on a **pilot scale** (e.g., one production line, one shift). Collect data:

$$\text{Pilot sample size: } n_{\text{pilot}} = 200 \text{ bearings}$$

$$\text{Defects observed: } x = 10 \text{ bearings}$$

$$\text{New defect rate} = p_{\text{new}} = \frac{10}{200} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — CHECK

Compare new defect rate against the hypothesis using a **two-proportion z-test**:

$$H_0: p_{\text{new}} = 0.08 \quad \text{(no improvement)}$$

$$H_1: p_{\text{new}} < 0.08 \quad \text{(improvement)}$$

Test statistic:

$$z = \frac{p_{\text{new}} - p_0}{\sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n_{\text{pilot}}}}}$$

Substituting:

$$z = \frac{0.05 - 0.08}{\sqrt{\frac{0.08 \times 0.92}{200}}}$$

$$z = \frac{-0.03}{\sqrt{\frac{0.0736}{200}}}$$

$$z = \frac{-0.03}{\sqrt{0.000368}}$$

$$z = \frac{-0.03}{0.01918}$$

$$z = -1.564$$

At significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), the critical value is $z_{\text{critical}} = -1.645$.

Since $z_{\text{calculated}} = -1.564 > -1.645$, we **fail to reject** $H_0$ at the 5% level, but the result is **marginally significant** (p-value ≈ 0.059). Deming would advocate continuing the cycle rather than declaring failure.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — ACT

Standardize the vibration-damping intervention across all grinding machines. Begin a **new PDCA cycle** to address the next-largest Pareto contributor (e.g., raw material inconsistency). This iterative application embodies Deming's principle of **constant improvement**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Deming's 14 Points Mapped to Manufacturing Strategy Functions

| Strategy Function | Relevant Deming Points | Strategic Outcome |
|--------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Procurement** | Point 4 (end lowest-price awarding) | Total cost of ownership reduction |
| **Production** | Points 3, 5 (build in quality, continuous improvement) | Defect rate reduction |
| **HR / Workforce** | Points 6, 8, 12, 13 (training, fear, pride, education) | Employee engagement and capability |
| **Sales / Marketing** | Point 1 (constancy of purpose) | Long-term customer value |
| **Finance** | Point 2 (new philosophy) | Long-term value over short-term profit |
| **Cross-Functional** | Point 9 (break down barriers) | Integrated supply chain |
| **Leadership** | Point 7 (institute leadership) | Transformational management |

## 3.3 Quantitative Summary Table — Variation Sources in Manufacturing

| Variation Type | Cause | Management Action | Deming's Term |
|----------------|-------|-------------------|----------------|
| Tool wear | Gradual | Predictive maintenance | Common cause |
| Power surge | Sudden spike | Surge protector, investigation | Special cause |
| Operator error | Procedural | Training (Point 6) | System cause (94%) |
| Material batch variability | Supplier | Supplier partnership (Point 4) | System cause (94%) |
| Machine breakdown | Random | Preventive maintenance | Special cause |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight for KTU Exams:** The examiner frequently tests whether students can distinguish **common cause from special cause variation**. Common cause requires **system redesign**; special cause requires **local investigation and removal**. Confusing the two is called **tampering**, which Deming identified as a major managerial sin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
