## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Toyota Production System. List its two main pillars and state the goal of TPS.**

**Model Answer:**

The Toyota Production System (TPS) is an integrated manufacturing management philosophy and operational framework developed by Taiichi Ohno, which seeks to eliminate all forms of waste (muda), unevenness (mura), and overburden (muri) in pursuit of the most efficient production method.

**The two main pillars are:**
1. **Just-in-Time (JIT)** — Producing only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed.
2. **Jidoka (Autonomation)** — Building in quality by detecting abnormalities and stopping the line immediately.

**The goal of TPS** is to deliver the highest quality products at the lowest cost and in the shortest lead time, while ensuring the best safety and morale for workers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Muda, Mura, and Muri. Which one is called the "mother of all wastes" and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Muda** | Waste — any activity that consumes resources without creating value | Defective products, excess inventory, waiting time |
| **Mura** | Unevenness — irregularity in production flow or demand | Producing 100 units on Monday and 0 on Tuesday |
| **Muri** | Overburden — unreasonable demands on people or machines | A worker lifting 50 kg when the design allows 20 kg |

**"Overproduction" (a form of Muda) is called the mother of all wastes** because producing more than the customer needs *creates* the other wastes: the excess parts must be stored (inventory waste), moved (transportation waste), and may become defective (defect waste), thus hiding every other inefficiency under a pile of unsold goods.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Explain the **Just-in-Time (JIT)** system in detail. Discuss its key components, working principles, and benefits. State the conditions that must be met for successful JIT implementation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory operates two shifts of 8 hours each. There is a 30-minute morning break, a 45-minute lunch break, and a 15-minute end-of-shift meeting per shift. The factory must produce 200 units per day. Calculate the **Takt Time**. If a workstation has a measured **Cycle Time** of 90 seconds, will the workstation meet demand? Justify with calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Just-in-Time (JIT) — Detailed Explanation:**

JIT is a pull-based production strategy in which each workstation produces only what the downstream (next) workstation needs, only when it needs it, and only in the quantity that the downstream station has signaled for. It is the operational embodiment of the **"right item, right quantity, right time"** philosophy.

**Key Components of JIT:**

1. **Pull System (Kanban):** Production is authorized by cards (Kanban) that travel *upstream* from the customer. A station only produces when it receives a Kanban card.
2. **Takt Time Discipline:** The pace of production is set to match the rate of customer demand.
3. **Continuous Flow (One-Piece Flow):** Parts move from station to station one at a time with no batching or queuing.
4. **Leveled Production (Heijunka):** Production volume and mix are smoothed across the day to avoid peaks and valleys.
5. **Quick Changeover (SMED):** Setup times are reduced to under 10 minutes so that small batches become economical.
6. **Stable, Reliable Suppliers:** Suppliers are treated as partners, delivering small lots multiple times per day.

**Working Principle:**
A customer order triggers a withdrawal signal at the final assembly line. The final line signals backward through the Kanban system. Each upstream station replenishes *only* the exact quantity withdrawn. This continues all the way back to the raw material supplier, who delivers in small, frequent batches. There is **no work in process** (WIP) sitting idle.

**Benefits of JIT:**
- Drastic reduction in inventory holding costs
- Shorter lead times and faster response to customers
- Higher quality (defects are caught immediately at the source)
- Reduced floor space and storage costs
- Lower work-in-progress reveals bottlenecks faster
- Improved worker engagement (visible problems, visible improvements)

**Conditions for Successful JIT Implementation:**
1. **Stable, level demand** — JIT fails under volatile demand.
2. **Reliable, high-quality suppliers** located geographically close to the plant.
3. **Standardized work** with low setup times.
4. **Empowered, multi-skilled workforce** capable of operating multiple machines.
5. **Strong supplier partnerships** based on long-term contracts, not price-only bidding.
6. **Tight integration** between engineering, production, and suppliers.
7. **Robust preventive maintenance** to avoid unplanned downtime.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Total Available Time per Day.**

The factory operates two shifts of 8 hours each. Each shift has a 30-min morning break, 45-min lunch, and 15-min end-of-shift meeting. **Total non-productive time per shift = 30 + 45 + 15 = 90 minutes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{per\,shift} &= 8 \times 60 - 90 = 480 - 90 = 390 \text{ min} \\
T_{per\,day} &= 2 \times 390 = 780 \text{ min} \\
T_{per\,day\,in\,sec} &= 780 \times 60 = 46{,}800 \text{ sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Takt Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}} \\
&= \dfrac{46{,}800 \text{ sec}}{200 \text{ units}} \\
&= 234 \text{ sec/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The Takt Time is 234 seconds per unit.**

**Step 3 — Compare Takt Time with Cycle Time.**

- Takt Time ($T_t$) = 234 sec/unit (demand rate)
- Cycle Time ($T_c$) = 90 sec/unit (station capability)

Since $T_c = 90 < T_t = 234$, the station produces one unit in 90 seconds, but the customer only needs one unit every 234 seconds.

**Step 4 — Compute Capacity Utilization and Idle Time.**

The station has slack capacity because it is faster than the Takt rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Utilization} &= \dfrac{T_c}{T_t} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{90}{234} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 38.46\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Idle Time per Unit} &= T_t - T_c = 234 - 90 = 144 \text{ sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The workstation **can meet demand**, and it has a substantial idle time of 144 seconds per unit. Under the TPS philosophy, this station could potentially be cross-trained to operate an additional machine, or work could be redistributed (Yamazumi) to balance the line.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Explain the **Jidoka** principle. Discuss its four-step response to an abnormality. How does Jidoka differ from traditional automation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC machining center operates with the following daily data:
- Planned Production Time: 600 minutes
- Unplanned Downtime: 90 minutes
- Ideal Cycle Time: 3 minutes/piece
- Total Pieces Produced: 150 pieces
- Good Pieces: 135 pieces

Calculate the **Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE** of the machine. Comment on whether the machine is operating at "World Class" level. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Jidoka — Detailed Explanation:**

The word *Jidoka* is the Japanese contraction of *Jido* (automation) and *Hō* (human). It is often translated as **"autonomation"** or **"automation with a human touch."** The principle was first articulated by Sakichi Toyoda when he invented the automatic loom in 1896: a thread-breakage detector that **stopped the machine automatically** rather than producing defective cloth.

**The Four-Step Response to an Abnormality:**

When any abnormality is detected (defect, machine malfunction, safety hazard, or delayed part), the Jidoka protocol requires:

1. **Detect the abnormality.** A sensor, a worker, or a Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) device notices that something is wrong.
2. **Stop the line immediately.** The worker pulls the Andon cord or presses the Andon button, halting the entire line. Production does **not** continue to "make up" for the lost time.
3. **Fix the root cause immediately.** The team, led by a team leader or engineer, identifies the **5-Why** cause and addresses it *at the moment*, not later.
4. **Install a countermeasure (Poka-Yoke).** A device or procedure is added so that the same abnormality can never recur.

**How Jidoka Differs from Traditional Automation:**

| Dimension | Traditional Automation | Jidoka (Autonomation) |
|---|---|---|
| **Response to defect** | Machine continues, defects flow downstream | Machine stops itself, defect is contained |
| **Quality control** | Separate inspection department | Built in at the workstation |
| **Worker role** | Operator (mindless task) | Inspector-operator (owns quality) |
| **Goal of automation** | Replace human labor | Amplify human judgment |
| **System reaction** | Push the bad product through | Pull the Andon, fix at source |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:** Many students write that Jidoka simply means "automation." **This is incorrect.** Jidoka is *not* about removing humans; it is about *giving machines the human ability to recognize and react to problems*. A fully automated machine that runs without anyone noticing defects is *not* practicing Jidoka.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Planned Production Time ($PPT$) = 600 min
- Unplanned Downtime ($DT$) = 90 min
- Ideal Cycle Time ($ICT$) = 3 min/piece
- Total Pieces Produced ($TP$) = 150 pieces
- Good Pieces ($GP$) = 135 pieces

**Step 1 — Compute Availability (A).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{PPT - DT}{PPT} \\
&= \dfrac{600 - 90}{600} \\
&= \dfrac{510}{600} \\
&= 0.850 \; \text{or} \; 85.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Performance (P).**

First, determine Run Time ($RT$):

$$
RT = PPT - DT = 600 - 90 = 510 \text{ min}
$$

Then compute Performance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{ICT \times TP}{RT} \\
&= \dfrac{3 \times 150}{510} \\
&= \dfrac{450}{510} \\
&= 0.882 \; \text{or} \; 88.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Quality (Q).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{GP}{TP} \\
&= \dfrac{135}{150} \\
&= 0.900 \; \text{or} \; 90.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \\
&= 0.850 \times 0.882 \times 0.900 \\
&= 0.6747 \\
&\approx 67.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on World-Class Level:**

The machine is **not** operating at world-class level. The generally accepted world-class benchmark for OEE is **85%** (set by Seiichi Nakajima). The machine in this case is at **67.5%**, which is approximately 17.5 percentage points below world class. The biggest loss is in **Quality (10% defect rate)** — 15 pieces out of 150 are defective, which is a strong indicator of a maintenance or process-capability problem. A Kaizen team should be chartered to address the quality loss first, since improving Quality typically has the highest ROI in OEE improvement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — OEE Calculation Errors:**
> 1. Students often confuse **Run Time** with **Planned Production Time**. Run Time = PPT − Downtime. Using PPT directly inflates the Performance metric incorrectly.
> 2. OEE is the **product** of A × P × Q, **not the average**. Do not use $(A + P + Q) / 3$.
> 3. Always convert the final decimal to a percentage for the answer (e.g., 0.675 → 67.5%). KTU deducts 0.5 marks for omitting the % sign.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density KTU Revision Block — Toyota Production System**

- **Definition:** TPS is a socio-technical system for eliminating waste (muda), unevenness (mura), and overburden (muri) to achieve the highest quality at the lowest cost in the shortest lead time.
- **Two Pillars:** (1) Just-in-Time (JIT) — pull-based production. (2) Jidoka (Autonomation) — build-in quality with automatic line stop.
- **Goal of TPS (Roof):** Highest Quality + Lowest Cost + Shortest Lead Time + Best Safety + Best Morale.
- **Foundation of TPS:** Standardized Work + Kaizen + Respect for People + Genchi Genbutsu.
- **The 3 Ms:** Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness), Muri (overburden).
- **The 7+1 Wastes:** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, + Unused Skills.
- **Mother of All Wastes:** Overproduction — because it hides all other wastes.
- **Takt Time Formula:** $T_t = T_{available} / D_{demand}$ (seconds per unit).
- **If Cycle Time > Takt Time:** The station is a bottleneck and cannot keep up with customer demand.
- **OEE Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ — multiplicative, not average.
- **World-Class OEE:** 85% (Nakajima benchmark).
- **Kanban:** Visual signal card that authorizes production or withdrawal in a pull system; only the downstream station issues a card.
- **SMED:** Single Minute Exchange of Die — changeover target under 10 minutes.
- **Heijunka:** Leveled production to smooth demand and avoid the bullwhip effect.
- **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proofing device that prevents a defect from occurring or passing downstream.
- **Andon:** Visual signal (light, cord, or board) used by a worker to call for help and stop the line.
- **Jidoka is not automation:** It is automation *with* human judgment — the ability to detect, stop, and fix.
- **5-Why Analysis:** Iterative root-cause technique where "Why?" is asked 5 times to reach the systemic cause.
- **Continuous Flow vs. Mass Production:** TPS favors one-piece flow; mass production favors large batches.
- **Pull vs. Push:** In TPS, work is pulled by demand; in mass production, work is pushed by schedule.
- **KTU Board Hot Topics:** Takt Time calculation, OEE calculation, 7 wastes, JIT vs. Jidoka distinction, Kanban working, Muda/Mura/Muri differentiation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Class Manufacturing and India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT723 - MANUFACTURING STRATEGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** World Class Manufacturing and India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 World Class Manufacturing (WCM) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is a strategic, integrated management philosophy that positions a manufacturing enterprise at the absolute pinnacle of global competitiveness by simultaneously pursuing **zero defects, zero waste, zero breakdowns, zero inventories, zero lead times, and zero accidents** across the entire value chain.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, WCM is defined as the pursuit of manufacturing excellence through a holistic blend of:

- **Total Quality Management (TQM)**
- **Just-In-Time (JIT) production systems**
- **Lean Manufacturing**
- **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**
- **Agile Manufacturing**
- **Six Sigma statistical process control**

The term "World Class" is not a marketing slogan — it is a measurable benchmark that compares an organization's operational metrics (cost, quality, delivery, flexibility, innovation) against the **top 10% of global performers in the same product category**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Schonberger (1986) and Hay & Hay are credited as the founding thinkers of WCM. The phrase embodies the philosophy of doing it right the first time, every time, at the lowest total cost, with maximum customer delight.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Olympic Athlete of Factories"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a manufacturing plant as an Olympic sprinter. A world-class sprinter does not merely run fast — they possess:
> - **Consistent form** (Standardized processes),
> - **Injury-free body** (Zero breakdowns/accidents),
> - **Energy efficiency** (Lean resource use),
> - **Continuous training** (Kaizen — continuous improvement),
> - **Mental toughness** (Culture & people involvement),
> - **Adaptability to different tracks** (Agility/Flexibility).
>
> Just as the sprinter measures every millisecond, a WCM plant measures every defect, every second of lead time, every percentage of OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness). It is not about being *good* — it is about being the **best in the world at your specific category**.

### 1.3 The Strategic Context of WCM in India

India's manufacturing sector contributes approximately **14–17% of GDP** (target: 25% under the National Manufacturing Policy, aligned with the "Make in India" initiative launched in September 2014). For India to leap from a service-led economy to a balanced goods-and-services powerhouse, WCM adoption is not optional — it is a national strategic imperative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key WCM Performance Metrics (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) ≥ 85%**
> - **First Pass Yield (FPY) ≥ 99%**
> - **On-Time Delivery (OTD) ≥ 98%**
> - **Inventory Turns ≥ 12 per year**
> - **Defect Rate (DPMO) ≤ 3.4 (Six Sigma)**
> - **Lead Time Reduction ≥ 70%**
> - **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) ≤ 1% of sales**

### 1.4 Why WCM Matters for India — The Strategic Drivers

1. **Global competitiveness** against China, Germany, Japan, USA.
2. **Employment generation** — manufacturing has the highest employment elasticity.
3. **Export earnings** — reduce trade deficit.
4. **Technology absorption & innovation** — Industry 4.0 integration.
5. **Sustainable manufacturing** — circular economy, ESG compliance.
6. **Supply chain resilience** — lessons from COVID-19 disruptions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Ten Pillars of World Class Manufacturing (Schonberger Framework)

The Schonberger-Hay framework identifies **10 foundational pillars** that a firm must master to qualify as World Class:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Pillars of WCM (High-Yield KTU Topic):**
>
> 1. **Customer First (Customer is the Next Process)** — Voice of Customer (VOC) drives every process.
> 2. **Quality at Source (Poka-Yoke / Mistake-Proofing)** — Defects prevented, not detected.
> 3. **Just-In-Time (JIT) Production** — Produce only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed.
> 4. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — Operators own their machines; OEE ≥ 85%.
> 5. **Total Quality Management (TQM)** — Statistical Process Control (SPC), PDCA cycles.
> 6. **Employee Involvement & Empowerment** — Suggestion schemes, Quality Circles.
> 7. **Agile Manufacturing** — Quick changeover (SMED), flexible capacity.
> 8. **Lean Six Sigma** — DMAIC methodology for variation reduction.
> 9. **Supply Chain Integration** — Vendor partnership, Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI).
> 10. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — 5S, Gemba Walks, PDCA.

### 2.2 The Logical Structure — "Why" and "How" of Each Pillar

| # | Pillar | The "Why" (Strategic Rationale) | The "How" (Tactical Tools) |
|---|--------|----------------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Customer First | Retention drives 80% of profit (Pareto) | VOC → CTQ → QFD House of Quality |
| 2 | Quality at Source | Rework costs 4× the original operation | Poka-Yoke devices, SPC charts, Andon |
| 3 | JIT | Inventory hides problems | Kanban, Pull system, Takt time |
| 4 | TPM | Downtime = lost capacity = lost revenue | OEE tracking, Autonomous Maintenance |
| 5 | TQM | Variation is the enemy of quality | DMAIC, 7 QC tools, ISO 9001 |
| 6 | Employee Involvement | 70% of problems are people-process | Suggestion systems, QC Circles |
| 7 | Agile Mfg. | Mass customization demand rising | SMED, cellular mfg., mixed-model lines |
| 8 | Lean Six Sigma | Waste + Variation = Cost of Poor Quality | DMAIC, Value Stream Mapping (VSM) |
| 9 | Supply Chain | 60% of value is in suppliers | Vendor partnerships, EDI, CPFR |
| 10 | Kaizen | Stagnation = competitive decay | PDCA, 5S, Gemba Kaizen |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Core WCM Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** Below is the high-yield formula cheat sheet for KTU 2024 Scheme Manufacturing Strategy examinations. Avoid using the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside table cells — use LaTeX `\vert` instead.

| Metric | Formula | Target (WCM) | Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|------|
| OEE | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | $\geq 0.85$ | dimensionless |
| Availability ($A$) | $A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | $\geq 0.90$ | ratio |
| Performance ($P$) | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | $\geq 0.95$ | ratio |
| Quality ($Q$) | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | $\geq 0.99$ | ratio |
| Takt Time | $\text{Takt} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | — | seconds/unit |
| Lead Time | $\text{LT} = \text{Processing Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Wait Time} + \text{Transport Time}$ | minimize | time units |
| Inventory Turns | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | $\geq 12$ | turns/year |
| First Pass Yield | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units (no rework)}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | $\geq 0.99$ | ratio |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | $\geq 0.95$ | ratio |
| DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}}$ | $\leq 3.4$ (6σ) | defects/million |
| Sigma Level | $\sigma = Z + 1.5$ | $\geq 6$ | sigma |
| SMED Time | $\text{SMED} = \text{Internal Setup} + \text{External Setup}$ | minimize | minutes |
| Cope's Rule | $\text{COPQ} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Quality Failures}}{\text{Total Sales}}$ | $\leq 0.01$ | ratio |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These formulas are applied in:
- **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — global benchmark of WCM.
- **Tata Steel's Jamshedpur plant** — first Indian firm to win the Deming Prize.
- **Maruti Suzuki** — JIT supplier integration.
- **Bosch India** — Industry 4.0 + WCM hybrid.
- **Bharat Electronics Limited (BEL)** — Six Sigma deployments.

### 2.5 India's Strategic Roadmap Toward WCM

India's journey toward WCM is structured around multiple government initiatives:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Indian Policy Initiatives (KTU 2024 Scheme Critical):**
>
> 1. **Make in India (2014)** — National manufacturing revival program.
> 2. **Skill India (2015)** — Workforce upskilling for Industry 4.0.
> 3. **Production Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme** — ₹1.97 lakh crore across 14 sectors (electronics, pharma, auto, textiles, etc.).
> 4. **National Manufacturing Policy (2011)** — Target 25% GDP share.
> 5. **SAMADHAN (Defense Manufacturing)** — Atmanirbhar Bharat push.
> 6. **India Semiconductor Mission (2021)** — ₹76,000 crore outlay.
> 7. **Ease of Doing Business reforms** — Single-window clearance.
> 8. **National Logistics Policy (2022)** — PM Gati Shakti for supply chain integration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A CNC machining cell operates for **480 minutes** in an 8-hour shift. Planned downtime for breaks and changeovers is **60 minutes**. The cell processes **400 units**. The ideal cycle time is **0.9 minutes/unit**. Of the 400 units, **380 are good pieces** (20 are defective).

**Step 1: Calculate Operating Time.**

$$\text{Operating Time} = \text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime Losses}$$

$$\text{Operating Time} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Availability ($A$).**

$$A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \frac{420}{480}$$

$$A = 0.875 = 87.5\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate Performance ($P$).**

$$P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} = \frac{0.9 \times 400}{420}$$

$$P = \frac{360}{420} = 0.8571 = 85.71\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate Quality ($Q$).**

$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} = \frac{380}{400}$$

$$Q = 0.95 = 95.00\%$$

**Step 5: Calculate OEE.**

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.875 \times 0.8571 \times 0.95$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.7126 \approx 71.26\%$$

**Step 6: WCM Benchmark Comparison.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmarking (Typical OEE Values):**
> - World Class: **≥ 85%**
> - Average Indian Industry: **50–60%**
> - This Plant: **71.26%** (Above average, below world class)
>
> **Gap Analysis:** $\Delta\text{OEE} = 85 - 71.26 = 13.74\%$ improvement opportunity.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for Multi-Stage Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A four-stage manufacturing process has the following First Pass Yields (FPY):
> - Stage 1 (Stamping): FPY₁ = 0.98
> - Stage 2 (Welding): FPY₂ = 0.96
> - Stage 3 (Painting): FPY₃ = 0.99
> - Stage 4 (Assembly): FPY₄ = 0.95

**Step 1: Apply the multiplicative RTY formula.**

$$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$\text{RTY} = 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.99 \times 0.95$$

**Step 3: Compute sequentially.**

$$\text{RTY} = (0.98 \times 0.96) \times (0.99 \times 0.95)$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.9408 \times 0.9405$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.8849 \approx 88.49\%$$

**Step 4: Convert to DPMO for 100 opportunities/unit.**

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - 0.8849 = 0.1151 = 11.51\%$$

**Step 5: DPMO Calculation (assuming 100 opportunities per unit).**

$$\text{DPMO} = 0.1151 \times 10^6 = 115{,}100 \text{ DPMO}$$

**Step 6: Sigma Level Estimation.**

$$\sigma \approx Z + 1.5 \approx 1.20 + 1.5 = 2.7\sigma$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse RTY with the simple average of FPYs. **RTY is multiplicative** — it captures the compounding effect of defects across stages. The simple average here would be 0.97, but the true RTY is only 0.8849. This is a 5× difference in defect interpretation!

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Takt Time for Demand-Responsive Production

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A factory operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each**, with **60 minutes** of total break time per shift. Customer demand is **240 units/day**.

**Step 1: Total available production time.**

$$\text{Available Time} = (8 \times 60 \times 2) - (60 \times 2)$$

$$\text{Available Time} = 960 - 120 = 840 \text{ minutes/day}$$

**Step 2: Convert to seconds.**

$$\text{Available Time} = 840 \times 60 = 50{,}400 \text{ seconds/day}$$

**Step 3: Apply Takt formula.**

$$\text{Takt Time} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$$

$$\text{Takt Time} = \frac{50{,}400}{240} = 210 \text{ seconds/unit}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every **210 seconds**, one unit must exit the production line to meet daily demand. If cycle time > 210s, demand cannot be met without overtime or capacity expansion.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — India vs. World Class Benchmarks

| Dimension | Japan (Toyota) | Germany (Industry 4.0) | China (Foxconn) | India (Current) | India (Target 2030) |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| OEE | 85–95% | 80–88% | 75–85% | 50–65% | 80% |
| Inventory Turns | 18–25 | 12–18 | 15–22 | 6–10 | 12 |
| Defect Rate (DPMO) | < 100 | < 500 | < 1000 | 3000–8000 | < 1000 |
| On-Time Delivery | 99%+ | 98%+ | 95%+ | 85–90% | 95%+ |
| Automation Level | Very High | Very High | High | Low–Medium | Medium–High |
| Skill Index (0–10) | 9 | 9 | 7 | 5 | 7 |
| R&D Spend (% GDP) | 3.3% | 3.1% | 2.4% | 0.65% | 2.0% |

### 3.5 SWOT Analysis — India's WCM Readiness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's WCM SWOT Matrix (Board Examination Favorite):**

**Strengths:**
- Largest young workforce (median age 28).
- Strong IT/ITeS ecosystem enabling smart manufacturing.
- Cost-competitive labor.

**Weaknesses:**
- Low R&D spend (0.65% of GDP vs. 3% global leaders).
- Fragmented MSMEs lacking scale for WCM adoption.
- Skill gap in advanced manufacturing.

**Opportunities:**
- China+1 supply chain diversification.
- PLI scheme incentives.
- Demographic dividend until 2055.

**Threats:**
- Global geopolitical instability.
- Competition from Vietnam, Indonesia, Bangladesh.
- Climate compliance costs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)**. List any **four core principles** that distinguish a WCM firm from a conventional manufacturer.

**Model Answer:**

World Class Manufacturing is a strategic philosophy that positions a firm among the global top performers in its product category by integrating quality, speed, cost, and flexibility excellence across the entire value chain.

Four core principles:

1. **Customer Focus** — Every process is designed to deliver customer value.
2. **Zero Defects Philosophy** — Quality is built in, not inspected in (Poka-Yoke, SPC).
3. **Just-In-Time (JIT)** — Eliminate all forms of waste (Muda, Mura, Muri).
4. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — Operator-owned equipment with OEE ≥ 85%.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for four principles)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Explain the concept of **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** with its three sub-components. Why is OEE the single most important metric in WCM?

**Model Answer:**

OEE measures the percentage of scheduled time that is truly productive.

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

- **Availability (A):** Actual run time ÷ Planned production time.
- **Performance (P):** Actual output speed ÷ Designed speed.
- **Quality (Q):** Good units ÷ Total units produced.

**Why OEE matters:** It captures the *Six Big Losses* (breakdowns, setup, idling, reduced speed, defects, startup yield) in one number. WCM firms target OEE ≥ 85%. Each 1% OEE improvement typically yields significant cost savings and capacity without capital investment.

*(3 marks: 1 mark formula + 1 mark three components + 1 mark importance)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — OPTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss the **Schonberger's Ten Pillars of World Class Manufacturing** in detail. Explain how each pillar contributes to operational excellence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** "India cannot achieve WCM without government policy support." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to the **Make in India initiative, PLI scheme, and Industry 4.0 readiness**. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL ANSWER (Option A)

**(a) Schonberger's Ten Pillars of WCM:**

1. **Customer as the Next Process** — Internal customers must receive defect-free output. Tools: VOC analysis, CTQ translation, QFD.
2. **Quality at the Source** — Workers prevent defects, not inspectors detect them. Tools: Poka-Yoke, Jidoka, SPC charts.
3. **Just-In-Time (JIT)** — Pull-based production triggered by real demand. Tools: Kanban, Takt time, Heijunka.
4. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — Operators maintain their own machines; OEE ≥ 85%. Eliminates Six Big Losses.
5. **TQM** — Continuous improvement embedded in culture. Tools: PDCA, 7 QC tools, ISO 9001.
6. **People Involvement** — 70% of problems are people-related. Tools: QC circles, suggestion systems.
7. **Agile Manufacturing** — Rapid response to demand changes. Tools: SMED, cellular layout.
8. **Lean Six Sigma** — Combine waste elimination (Lean) + variation reduction (Six Sigma).
9. **Supply Chain Integration** — Suppliers are partners, not adversaries. Tools: VMI, CPFR, EDI.
10. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Small daily improvements compound into breakthrough results.

**Contribution:** Each pillar addresses a specific waste category — defects, inventory, motion, waiting, transport, over-processing, over-production. Together they create a self-reinforcing system of operational excellence.

**(b) Evaluation of the Statement:**

The statement is **largely correct** but requires nuance. Government policy is necessary but not sufficient.

**In Favor (Policy Support is Critical):**

- **Make in India (2014):** Brand-building campaign attracting FDI; production growth from $390B (2014) to $620B (2023).
- **PLI Scheme:** ₹1.97 lakh crore outlay across 14 sectors — electronics production grew from $30B to $100B+ in 5 years.
- **Industry 4.0:** National AI strategy, semiconductor mission (₹76,000 crore), 5G rollout enabling smart factories.
- **Ease of Doing Business:** India jumped from 142nd (2014) to 63rd (2022) in World Bank rankings.

**Against (Policy Alone is Insufficient):**

- **Cultural barriers:** Hierarchical mindsets, fear of failure, low employee empowerment.
- **Skill gap:** Only 2.3% of Indian workforce has formal vocational training vs. 75% in Germany.
- **MSME fragmentation:** 63 million MSMEs lack capital for WCM adoption.
- **R&D deficit:** 0.65% of GDP vs. global benchmark 2–3%.

**Verdict:** Policy creates the **enabling environment**, but execution depends on **management commitment, workforce capability, and cultural transformation**. Both must work in tandem.

*(14 marks: 7 + 7)*

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — OPTION B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **concept of OEE**. A CNC cell operates for **450 minutes** with **40 minutes of downtime**, produces **500 units** at an **ideal cycle time of 0.85 minutes**, with **480 good units**. Calculate the OEE and comment on its WCM compliance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Examine the role of **Lean Manufacturing, Six Sigma, and TPM** in India's journey toward WCM. Discuss any **two case studies of Indian companies** that have successfully implemented these tools. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL ANSWER (Option B)

**(a) OEE Calculation:**

**Step 1: Planned Production Time** = 450 min (already given).

**Step 2: Operating Time** = 450 − 40 = 410 minutes.

**Step 3: Availability:**

$$A = \frac{410}{450} = 0.9111 = 91.11\%$$

**Step 4: Performance:**

$$P = \frac{0.85 \times 500}{410} = \frac{425}{410} = 1.0366$$

Since P > 1 is unusual (the cell is exceeding ideal cycle), we cap it at 100%:

$$P = 1.00 = 100\%$$

**Step 5: Quality:**

$$Q = \frac{480}{500} = 0.96 = 96.00\%$$

**Step 6: OEE:**

$$\text{OEE} = 0.9111 \times 1.00 \times 0.96 = 0.8747 \approx 87.47\%$$

**WCM Compliance:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OEE = 87.47% ≥ 85%** — The cell **qualifies as World Class**. However, the quality component (96%) drags the score down. Recommendation: Implement Poka-Yoke and SPC to push Q above 99% and OEE above 90%.

**(b) Role of Lean, Six Sigma, TPM + Indian Case Studies:**

**Lean Manufacturing in India:**
- Eliminates 8 wastes (DOWNTIME: Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transport, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing).
- **Case Study 1: Tata Steel** — Implemented Lean across Jamshedpur plant; reduced inventory by ₹1,200 crore; won Deming Prize (2012), Japan's highest quality award.

**Six Sigma in India:**
- Reduces variation using DMAIC (Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control).
- **Case Study 2: Wipro Technologies** — Deployed Six Sigma across IT and BPO operations; saved $200M+ in 10 years; won the Golden Peacock Award.

**Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) in India:**
- Operators own equipment maintenance; autonomous maintenance pillars.
- **Case Study 3: Maruti Suzuki India** — Implemented TPM across Manesar plant; OEE jumped from 65% to 88%; defect rate dropped 70%; recognized as TPM Excellence Award winner by JIPM (Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance).

**Conclusion:** When Lean + Six Sigma + TPM converge under a unified WCM framework, Indian manufacturers can compete globally. The convergence approach is called "**Lean Six Sigma with TPM**" — increasingly adopted by Bosch India, Ashok Leyland, and Mahindra & Mahindra.

*(14 marks: 7 + 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Conflating TQM with WCM:** TQM is a *subset* of WCM. WCM = TQM + Lean + JIT + TPM + Six Sigma + Agile Manufacturing.
> 2. **OEE Calculation Errors:** Students often forget to cap Performance at 100%. A common mistake is P > 1 without acknowledging that OEE is bounded between 0 and 1.
> 3. **Missing WCM benchmarks:** Always state the **target value** alongside the metric (e.g., OEE ≥ 85%, FPY ≥ 99%).
> 4. **No Indian Context:** KTU examiners award bonus marks for **referencing Indian examples** (Tata Steel, Maruti, Wipro, Ashok Leyland, BEL).
> 5. **Generic answers:** Avoid writing about "ISO certification" only — WCM is much broader and includes industry 4.0 dimensions.
> 6. **Diagram requirement:** For 7-mark sub-parts, always include a **neat labelled diagram or block schematic** — 1–2 marks are reserved for visual presentation.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Important Things to Remember — Module 4: World Class Manufacturing and India**

- ✅ **Definition:** WCM = Strategic integration of TQM + JIT + TPM + Lean + Six Sigma + Agile Manufacturing to achieve world-class benchmarks.

- ✅ **10 Pillars (Schonberger):** Customer Focus, Quality at Source, JIT, TPM, TQM, Employee Involvement, Agile Mfg, Lean Six Sigma, Supply Chain Integration, Kaizen.

- ✅ **Core Formula:**
$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q \quad \text{(target} \geq 85\% \text{)}$$

- ✅ **Six Sigma Threshold:** DPMO ≤ 3.4 corresponds to 6σ quality level.

- ✅ **Takt Time:** Available production time ÷ Customer demand (controls line rhythm).

- ✅ **RTY is multiplicative** — not the simple average of FPYs.

- ✅ **SMED = Single Minute Exchange of Dies** — minimize setup time for agile manufacturing.

- ✅ **Make in India + PLI + Industry 4.0 + Skill India + Gati Shakti** = India's WCM policy stack.

- ✅ **OEE Interpretation:** < 65% (Poor) | 65–75% (Average) | 75–85% (Good) | ≥ 85% (World Class).

- ✅ **Indian WCM Benchmarks:** Tata Steel (Deming Prize), Maruti Suzuki (TPM), Wipro (Six Sigma), Bosch India (Industry 4.0).

- ✅ **WCM ≠ ISO Certification** — ISO is a baseline compliance; WCM is continuous excellence.

- ✅ **India's Target:** Raise manufacturing GDP share from 14% → 25% by 2030.

- ✅ **Five-Zero Vision:** Zero defects, Zero breakdowns, Zero inventory, Zero lead time, Zero accidents.

- ✅ **Always cite Indian examples** in KTU answers for maximum marks.

- ✅ **WCM Maturity:** Reactive → Stabilized → Defined → Managed → World Class (5-level progression).

- ✅ **Cultural transformation** is the hardest pillar — technology is the easiest.
